planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in fall in well-drained, acidic soil slightly above grade; fall planting allows root establishment before the autumn and winter bloom season begins.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in partial shade to full sun; sasanqua camellias bloom in fall and early winter, earlier than Camellia japonica, and are more sun-tolerant and heat-hardy, making them the better choice for Gulf Coast gardens in zones 8–9. Single, semi-double, or double flowers in white, pink, red, and lavender provide vivid color from October through January when little else is in bloom. Unlike japonicas, sasanquas can be trained as espaliers against walls, used as hedges, or even grown as small specimen trees. Apply pine-bark mulch and acid-forming camellia fertilizer in spring. Petal blight and scale are the main concerns; improve air circulation and treat scale with horticultural oil in late winter.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost, or in fall in zones 8–9; loropetalum establishes quickly in warm soil and rewards with rapid growth during the first growing season with adequate moisture.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; loropetalum is one of the most popular landscape shrubs for the Gulf Coast and Southeast, prized for its burgundy to purple-bronze foliage that holds color year-round and its showy fringe-like flowers in spring and sporadically through the year. The purple-leaved forms ('Plum Delight', 'Burgundy', 'Purple Diamond') are especially striking contrasted against green neighbors. It tolerates summer heat and humidity better than most ornamental shrubs. Prune immediately after the main spring flowering to shape; do not shear into tight balls — this destroys the natural graceful arching habit and removes flowering wood. Apply a slow-release, acid-forming fertilizer in early spring.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall; beautyberry establishes quickly from container stock and begins producing berries the first or second year after planting.

directions: Plant in well-drained to average soil in full sun to partial shade; American beautyberry is a native Gulf Coast shrub with large, coarse leaves and absolutely stunning clusters of metallic magenta-purple berries in fall that persist after leaf drop and create a brilliant late-season display. It is native to the forests, clearings, and forest edges of the Southeast and is uniquely tolerant of heat, drought, and sandy soil. The berries are eaten by 40+ species of birds and the crushed leaves contain callicarpenal, which has documented mosquito-repellent properties. Prune hard (to 12 inches) in late winter every few years to rejuvenate; beautyberry fruits on new wood, so hard pruning stimulates vigorous growth and heavy fruiting. The white-berried cultivar 'Lactea' is also striking.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring; spicebush is dioecious — plant both male and female shrubs for berry production. One male plant is sufficient to pollinate 4–5 female plants within 50 feet.

directions: Plant in moist, average to rich soil in partial to full shade; northern spicebush is one of the few large shrubs truly native to the forest understory, making it invaluable for shaded landscapes. The tiny yellow flowers in early spring appear before the leaves and provide early nectar for native bees; the brilliantly yellow fall foliage is outstanding. Bright red, oil-rich berries on female plants in fall are devoured by migratory thrushes and are the primary larval host plant for the spicebush swallowtail butterfly. The dried berries are edible with a complex allspice-bay-pepper flavor once used as a colonial-era spice — they can replace allspice in recipes. All parts are intensely aromatic when crushed.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring after last frost in well-drained soil; in zone 7–8, plant against a south-facing wall for warmth and protection. Container culture is excellent for bringing indoors during harsh winters.

directions: Plant in well-drained, average to sandy soil in full sun; Celeste is the most popular and cold-hardy fig in the Deep South, producing small to medium, violet-brown figs with a honey-sweet, rose-pink interior that ripen mid-July through August. It is the standard home garden fig from Virginia to Texas and is considered by many to have the best flavor of any common fig variety. Figs fruit on second-year wood and on new growth late in the season — do not prune heavily in winter. Mulch roots in zone 7 to prevent root freeze-kill. Trees rarely need fertilizer and will produce heavy crops with minimal care once established. Other excellent Gulf Coast cultivars include 'Brown Turkey', 'LSU Purple', and 'LSU Gold'.

planting.note: Plant in spring after last frost; Brown Turkey establishes quickly and often produces the first small crop in year two. Choose a site with full sun and excellent drainage — the roots do not tolerate standing water.

directions: Plant in well-drained, average soil in full sun; Brown Turkey is among the most widely grown figs in the South and UK, valued for its large, mild, brownish-purple fruits and its exceptional vigor. It is a two-crop fig, producing a small breba crop in June on old wood and a main crop in late summer on new wood. The tree grows vigorously and can reach 15–20 feet unpruned — maintain at 8–10 feet by annual tipping of tall canes in winter. The large, deeply lobed leaves provide striking tropical texture in the landscape. Brown Turkey is more tolerant of cool summers than most figs and produces reliably even when summer heat is limited, making it popular well north of its zone rating when given wall protection.
sources: University of California Agriculture and Natural Resources

planting.note: Plant dormant bare-root vines in early spring; self-fertile cultivars ('Carlos', 'Noble', 'Ison') can be planted singly, but planting a bronze and a black variety together often increases yield. Train on a sturdy trellis — mature vines can exceed 100 feet in length.

directions: Plant in well-drained, loamy to sandy soil in full sun; muscadine is the native grape of the Southeast, uniquely resistant to Pierce's disease, downy mildew, and black rot that devastate European and California wine grapes in the humid South. Thick-skinned, musky, intensely flavored fruits ripen in clusters of 3–8 in August through October in black, bronze, red, and golden varieties. Muscadine grapes are high in antioxidants and polyphenols. Prune dormant vines severely (to 2–4 buds per spur) in late winter every year for maximum production — unpruned vines fruit sparsely. Prune cuts bleed sap profusely; this is normal. Self-fertile cultivars like 'Carlos', 'Ison', and 'Summit' are the most reliable for home gardens.

planting.note: Plant grafted cultivars (which fruit earlier than seedlings) in early spring; mayhaw tolerates wet, poorly drained, even periodically flooded soil and grows naturally in swampy bottomland — it is one of the few fruit trees adapted to wet clay.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, acidic, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; mayhaw is a small native hawthorn of Gulf Coast swamps and river bottoms that produces brilliant white flowers in late winter or early spring, followed by small red or yellow crabapple-like fruits that ripen in April–May (hence 'May' haw). The fruits are harvested by spreading tarps beneath trees and shaking, or collected floating in flooded lowlands, then processed into the iconic mayhaw jelly of Gulf Coast tradition — bright pink-orange, uniquely flavored, and prized at farm stands throughout Louisiana, Texas, and Georgia. Grafted cultivars (Big Red, Super Berry, Crimson) produce much heavier crops than wild seedlings. Trees need no special care once established.

planting.note: Plant rhizome clumps or container plants in spring after frost danger passes; set the crown just at soil level in a generous planting hole. In zones 7–8, plant against a south-facing wall and mulch heavily in winter.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; agapanthus produces strappy, strap-like evergreen foliage and tall, sturdy flower stems topped by globose umbels of blue, purple, or white trumpet-shaped flowers in June–July that are extraordinarily striking in mass plantings. It is one of the signature plants of Gulf Coast landscapes, thriving in heat, humidity, and coastal conditions with very little care. Clumps become slightly root-bound over time and actually flower more prolifically when congested — do not divide unless absolutely necessary. Deciduous types (A. praecox) are more cold-hardy to zone 7 with protection. Deadhead spent stems for neatness and to prevent abundant self-seeding of named cultivars.

planting.note: Divide clumps or plant container starts in spring; establishes quickly and begins blooming within the first season. In zone 8, mulch heavily for winter protection.

directions: Plant in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun; society garlic is a South African native perfectly adapted to Gulf Coast heat and drought, producing strappy blue-green foliage and clusters of lavender-pink flowers almost continuously from spring through fall. Despite the name, it lacks the offensive breath component of garlic while retaining a mild garlic flavor — the leaves, flowers, and bulbs are all edible and used in salads, stir-fries, and as a cooked vegetable in South African cuisine. It is a superb low-maintenance edging plant for sunny borders, extremely tolerant of heat, reflected heat, poor soil, and dry conditions once established. Deer and rabbits avoid it completely. Divide congested clumps every 3–4 years in spring.
